label: Art of the Veil 01
title: Art of the Veil #01 — First Crystal Study
alt: Side-by-side comparison of a rough and refined pixel art crystal used as a selection indicator in a tactical RPG
summary: First pixel art study for Veilkeeper, showing the evolution of a selection crystal from a rough initial shape to a more refined and readable form.

sections.text: As development continues, I’ve started learning pixel art alongside building the game. This is the first original asset created specifically for Veilkeeper — a selection crystal used in the combat grid.

The goal wasn’t to make something perfect, but to create something readable and functional that could fit into the game’s visual language. The crystal is designed in grayscale so it can be tinted later based on context, such as player actions, enemy states, or signal strength.

The difference between the two versions highlights a few key improvements: clearer shape definition, better separation of light and shadow, and a stronger overall silhouette. Even small adjustments to shading made a noticeable impact on readability.

This process is part of a larger effort to build a consistent visual identity for Veilkeeper. Early assets like this won’t be final, but they establish direction and make it easier to refine or replace them later with more polished work.
What improved

items: More defined light and shadow planes
Better foundation for future UI elements

sections.text: There’s still a long way to go, but this is the point where the visual side of the game starts to feel real. Future Art of the Veil posts will continue to explore UI elements, assets, and the evolving look of the game.

label: Devlog 05
title: Turn Readability & Unit States
alt: Units dim after acting with clearer turn flow in combat
summary: Combat readability improves with clearer turn flow and units dimming after acting.

sections.text: Combat readability continues to improve as the tactical loop takes shape. Units now visually dim after they’ve acted, making it easier to see which units are still available during a turn.
This also helps clarify enemy turns. With multiple units acting in sequence, the battlefield is easier to follow and the flow of combat is more understandable at a glance.
These changes don’t add new mechanics, but they make the existing systems much clearer. As more interactions are added, readability will become increasingly important.
Current progress
Next up is continuing to improve combat feedback and adding stronger indicators for targeting and damage resolution.

label: Devlog 04
title: Attacks & Unit Death
alt: Units attacking and being defeated in combat prototype
summary: Units can now attack and be defeated. The first full combat interaction is now working.

sections.text: Units can now attack and be defeated on the battlefield. This marks the first complete combat interaction in Veilkeeper’s tactical layer.
With selection and movement already in place, this update adds targeting and damage resolution. Units can now engage enemies, reduce health, and remove defeated units from the board.
This is the first point where the prototype starts to resemble an actual combat loop instead of isolated systems. Movement, positioning, and interaction now work together.
Current progress
Next up is improving feedback and readability as the combat loop continues to take shape.

label: Devlog 03
title: Unit Selection & Movement
alt: Unit selection and grid movement prototype
summary: Units can now be selected and moved across the grid. This establishes the foundation for turn flow, pathing, and future combat interactions.

sections.text: Big early milestone for Veilkeeper: units can now be selected and moved across the grid.
This builds on the cursor and input work from the last update, and it is the first point where the prototype really starts to feel like a tactics game instead of just a board with a highlight.
Current progress
Next up
Interaction on top of movement as I keep building toward the first full combat exchange.

label: Devlog 02
title: Cursor & Grid Interaction
summary: Grid highlighting now supports mouse, keyboard, and controller. Input handling is in place, and the board is starting to feel responsive.

sections.text: Work on Veilkeeper’s tactical cursor and grid interaction is now in place. The board can be navigated using mouse, keyboard, and controller, bringing the prototype closer to feeling like a playable tactics game.

This update focuses on responsiveness and readability. The cursor highlights grid spaces clearly, and input handling is designed to feel consistent regardless of control method. Establishing this early helps ensure that later mechanics build on something intuitive.

Even though the systems are still simple, this stage is important. The cursor is the player’s primary way of interacting with the battlefield, and getting that feeling right makes every future system easier to work with.
Current progress in this stage:
Next up is attaching this interaction to actual units. Selection and movement will build on this foundation and move the prototype closer to a full tactical combat loop.

label: Devlog 01
title: Foundations
summary: Veilkeeper’s tactical layer is taking shape. The current focus is building a clean combat foundation that can support the larger game over time.

sections.text: Development on Veilkeeper is starting with the core tactical layer. The goal is to build the game from the combat foundation outward, making sure each new system has something solid to sit on.

Right now, the focus is on establishing the grid, input handling, and the basic structure of turn-based interactions. This stage is less about visuals and more about making sure the game feels responsive and predictable.

The early prototype is centered around clarity. Movement, positioning, and board state should always be easy to understand at a glance. Even small improvements here make a big difference later when abilities, enemies, and systems begin stacking on top.
Current progress in this stage:

The next step is improving cursor control and interaction across different input methods. Once the board can be navigated cleanly, the focus will shift toward selecting units and moving them across the grid.
